title: Session Lifecycle And Active Connection
summary: Keep the user connected with server-managed refresh and clear client-side recovery states.
purpose: The app should preserve a valid session without exposing refresh tokens or forcing unnecessary logins.
rule: Maintain active sessions through server-side refresh using secure cookies. The client may trigger lightweight session checks on focus, resume, or important navigation, but it must not own refresh tokens.
whenToUse: Handling app focus, mobile resume, or long-running tabs.
Recovering from expired sessions during API calls.
whenNotToUse: Do not store refresh tokens in client-readable storage.
Do not refresh on every render.
Do not keep a dead session alive after logout or provider revocation.
Do not hide session-expired errors behind endless retries.

recommended.title: Server-owned refresh lifecycle

avoid.title: Client owns long-lived credentials

why: A session is a server trust relationship, not a client preference. Keeping refresh server-side means XSS cannot steal long-lived credentials from localStorage or a JavaScript store. Client-side lifecycle hooks are still useful, but they should only ask the server to validate or refresh the session and then react to the result.
checklist: Refresh tokens are never stored in localStorage, sessionStorage, or client state.
Session refresh happens through a server function or route handler.
Focus/resume checks are throttled and lightweight.
Expired sessions show a clear re-authentication path.
Logout clears cookies and invalidates server-side session state.
